root.narration: Bottom of the 5th, you're down 3-2 with a runner on second and one out. The count is 3-0. The pitcher has been wild all game -- 5 walks through 4 innings. Your coach has given you the green light, meaning you CAN swing if you get your pitch. The analytics say 3-0 fastballs are the most hittable pitch in baseball -- hitters slug .730 on 3-0 fastballs in the zone. But a walk puts runners on first and second with one out, and the pitcher is clearly struggling. Do you swing or take?

choices.text: Swing if it's a fastball in my zone! 3-0 fastball is the best pitch I'll see all day. The pitcher HAS to throw a strike -- I know exactly what's coming.
Take the pitch. If it's a ball, I walk and we have two runners on. If it's a strike, it's only 3-1 and I'm still way ahead in the count.
Look for one specific pitch in one specific zone. If I get a fastball middle-in, I'm swinging. Anything else, I take. Ultra-selective green light.

decision-swing-result.narration: The pitcher delivers -- it's a fastball, 89 mph, belt-high on the outer half. It's in the zone but not in your power zone. It's hittable but not ideal. You were looking for something more middle or in. Do you pull the trigger or hold up?

choices.text: It's a 3-0 fastball in the zone -- I'm swinging! Can't get much better than this.
It's not my pitch. Take it for a strike. I'm still 3-1 with the advantage.

decision-take-result.narration: You take the 3-0 pitch. The pitcher delivers -- fastball RIGHT down the middle, 88 mph, belt-high. The pitch you dream about. Strike one, 3-1. You watched the most hittable pitch of your at-bat go by. Now the pitcher has some confidence back -- he threw a strike. The next pitch is a changeup on the outside corner. 3-2. Full count. What's your approach now?

choices.text: Full count -- I have to protect the plate. Anything close, I'm swinging. Can't take a called strike three.
Stay disciplined. He's wild. If it's not in my zone, I take. I'd rather walk than chase a bad pitch.

decision-selective-result.narration: You're looking for ONE pitch: fastball middle-in. The pitcher delivers -- it's a fastball, middle-in, 88 mph, exactly where you were looking! This is the pitch! You swing with full confidence because you were looking for exactly this pitch in exactly this location. You drive it hard into the gap in right-center!

choices.text: The ball splits the gap! RBI double! Runner scores easily from second!

outcome-swing-outer-good.narration: You swing at the 3-0 fastball on the outer half. You make contact but you're a little out in front and roll over it -- ground ball to the shortstop! He throws to first for the out. Runner advances to third. Two outs. You swung at a pitch that was in the zone but not in your POWER zone. The 3-0 green light isn't about swinging at every strike -- it's about swinging at YOUR pitch. A belt-high fastball on the outer half is hittable, but it's not the pitch you crush.

outcome.headline: Ground Out. Swung at a Strike, Not YOUR Pitch.
outcome.explanation: The 3-0 green light is the most misunderstood concept in hitting. Having the green light doesn't mean 'swing at anything in the zone.' It means 'swing if you get YOUR pitch in YOUR zone.' Every hitter has a power zone -- the area of the strike zone where they do the most damage. For most hitters, that's middle-in, belt-high. The outer-half fastball is a strike, but it's not where you do your best work. On 3-0, you have the ultimate luxury: you KNOW a fastball is coming, and you can afford a strike because you're still ahead at 3-1. Use that luxury to be ultra-selective. Swing at the pitch you CRUSH, not the pitch that's technically hittable.
outcome.whatToRemember: The 3-0 green light means swing at YOUR pitch, not any strike. You can afford to take a strike -- use that leverage to be ultra-selective and only swing at pitches in your power zone.

outcome-hold-up-great.narration: You hold up -- 3-1. Smart! That pitch was in the zone but on the outer half, not in your power zone. Now at 3-1, the pitcher is still in trouble. He throws another fastball, this time middle-in -- YOUR pitch. You unload and drive it into the right-center gap! RBI double! The runner from second scores easily! Tie game, 3-3! By holding on the 3-0 pitch that wasn't in your zone, you got a BETTER pitch on 3-1 that you could really drive. Patience plus selectivity equals damage.

outcome.headline: RBI Double on 3-1! Patience Paid Off!
outcome.explanation: This is advanced hitting IQ. You had the green light on 3-0 but recognized the pitch wasn't in your power zone. Instead of swinging at a hittable-but-not-ideal pitch, you took the strike to get to 3-1 -- still a hitter's count where the pitcher MUST throw a strike. And on 3-1, you got the pitch you wanted and crushed it. The data supports this approach: while 3-0 fastballs in the zone are slugged at .730, the difference between a ball in your power zone versus the outer edge is enormous. Power-zone 3-0 swings produce slugging percentages above .900. Outer-half 3-0 swings produce slugging around .550. Being selective on 3-0 doesn't mean you don't swing -- it means you swing at the RIGHT pitch.
outcome.whatToRemember: Even with the green light on 3-0, be ultra-selective. A 3-1 count is still a massive advantage. Wait for a pitch in your power zone rather than swinging at anything the pitcher throws in the strike zone.

keyTerms.definition: Permission from the coach to swing on a 3-0 count, which traditionally is an automatic take. The green light is earned -- only hitters with good discipline and the ability to identify their pitch get it.
The specific area of the strike zone where an individual hitter does the most damage. Usually middle-in, belt-high for most hitters. Knowing your power zone is essential for 3-0 decisions.
A count where the pitcher must throw a strike to avoid falling further behind (3-0, 3-1, 2-0). Hitters perform dramatically better in these counts because they can sit on one pitch type.

outcome-take-protect-okay.narration: Full count. You expand your zone to protect the plate. The pitcher throws a slider that starts in the zone and breaks down and away. You swing -- barely catch it with the end of the bat. Weak ground ball to second, easy out. Runner stays at second, two outs. You chased a pitcher's pitch because you felt like you HAD to swing at anything close on a full count. But that slider was never going to be a strike -- it broke out of the zone. If you'd held your discipline, it would have been ball four.

outcome.headline: Ground Out. Expanded Zone on Full Count Cost You.
outcome.explanation: Expanding your zone on a full count against a wild pitcher is a mistake. You went from having a 3-0 advantage (where the pitcher HAS to groove a fastball) to a 3-2 chase (where the pitcher threw his best breaking ball and you chased it). The pitcher went from 'survival mode' at 3-0 to 'attack mode' at 3-2, and you played right into it. A wild pitcher with 5 walks in 4 innings is more likely to throw ball four on 3-2 than to paint a corner. Trust his wildness. If you had taken the 3-0 pitch for a strike and then laid off the slider, you would have walked. Sometimes the best swing is no swing.
outcome.whatToRemember: Against a wild pitcher, your best weapon is your discipline. Don't expand your zone on full counts -- a wild pitcher is more likely to walk you than to paint a corner. Let his wildness be your base hit.

outcome-take-discipline-good.narration: Full count. You stay disciplined. The pitcher throws a fastball on the outside corner -- the umpire calls it... BALL FOUR! Walk! You trot to first base. Runners on first and second, one out. The pitcher slams his glove -- he's unraveling. The next hitter drives a double to the gap and both runners score! You tied the game by taking your walk, and the rally continued because you got on base. The walk isn't glamorous, but it extended the inning and set up the big hit.

outcome.headline: Walk! Rally Continues! Discipline Wins!
outcome.explanation: Taking the walk against a struggling pitcher is often the highest-leverage offensive play. Here's why: 1) You got on base, which is the single most important offensive act. 2) You put more pressure on a pitcher who's already cracking. 3) You extended the inning for the hitters behind you. 4) The walk added a runner, turning a runner-on-second-one-out situation into runners-on-first-and-second-one-out -- which has HIGHER run expectancy. The only downside: you watched a 3-0 meatball go by. If that 3-0 pitch was in your power zone, swinging might have produced an extra-base hit. But taking the guaranteed base (the walk) versus gambling on one swing is a strong analytical choice, especially when the pitcher is wild.
outcome.whatToRemember: Against a wild pitcher, the walk is often your best outcome. It extends the inning, adds baserunners, and increases the pressure on a pitcher who's already struggling. Discipline is an offensive weapon.

outcome-selective-hit-great.narration: The ball splits the gap in right-center! Your runner from second scores standing up -- tie game! You cruise into second with an RBI double! The dugout erupts! You knew EXACTLY what pitch you were looking for and EXACTLY where you wanted it. When it arrived, there was zero hesitation -- pure confidence, pure conviction, maximum bat speed. That's the power of the ultra-selective green light: you eliminate all doubt and swing with 100% commitment because the pitch matches your predetermined plan perfectly.

outcome.headline: RBI DOUBLE! Ultra-Selective 3-0 Swing Perfection!
outcome.explanation: The ultra-selective approach on 3-0 is the highest-IQ version of the green light. Here's why it works: 1) You decided BEFORE the pitch exactly what you were looking for (fastball middle-in). 2) This eliminated the 'should I swing?' decision, which takes precious milliseconds away from your reaction time. 3) When the pitch matched your predetermined plan, you swung with maximum commitment and bat speed. 4) The pitcher was almost guaranteed to throw a fastball in the zone on 3-0, so your targeted zone was the most likely pitch to arrive. Research shows that hitters who have a pre-determined plan on 3-0 produce higher exit velocities and better launch angles than hitters who react in real-time. Knowing what you want BEFORE the pitch is the ultimate competitive edge.
outcome.whatToRemember: The best 3-0 approach is: pick ONE pitch in ONE zone before the pitch is thrown. If it arrives, swing with full conviction. If it doesn't, take the strike -- you're still ahead. Pre-determination eliminates hesitation.

keyTerms.definition: Deciding what pitch and location you will swing at BEFORE the pitch is delivered. Eliminates in-flight decision-making and allows maximum commitment to the swing.
A swing where the hitter has zero doubt, producing maximum bat speed and optimal contact. Only possible when the hitter has decided in advance that THIS is the pitch to attack.
On 3-0, the hitter knows a fastball is coming 80%+ of the time. This foreknowledge, combined with zone selection, creates the most advantageous pitch-recognition scenario in baseball.
